meta.title: LiteParse Explained
meta.subtitle: Fast, local document parsing for AI agents
meta.author: Imbila.AI

summary: LiteParse is an open-source document parsing library by LlamaIndex that extracts text from PDFs, Office documents, and images while preserving spatial layout. It runs entirely locally with no cloud calls, no API keys, and no LLMs involved in parsing. TypeScript-native with built-in OCR via Tesseract.js, it is designed specifically for AI agent workflows that need fast, reliable document reading.

products.name: Spatial Text Parsing
products.description: Projects text onto a spatial grid using whitespace and indentation to preserve original page layout. Instead of converting tables and columns to Markdown (which breaks constantly), it keeps spatial relationships intact and lets the LLM interpret what it sees.

products.name: Bounding Boxes
products.description: Every line of extracted text includes precise coordinate data — where it sat on the page, how wide it was. Useful for downstream processing, visualization, or building region-specific extraction pipelines.

products.name: Built-in OCR
products.description: Scanned PDFs and images are handled automatically via Tesseract.js. OCR parallelises across CPU cores by default (num_workers = cores - 1). Supports plugging in external OCR servers (PaddleOCR, EasyOCR) for higher accuracy on difficult documents.

products.name: Multimodal Screenshots
products.description: Generates page-level screenshots alongside text output. Enables a two-step agent pattern: parse text for fast understanding, fall back to screenshots when the agent needs to visually inspect charts, diagrams, or complex formatting.

local_parsing.description: Open-source, local-first. Spatial text + bounding boxes + screenshots. Fast, simple, no cloud. Best for agents and real-time pipelines where speed matters more than perfect structure detection.

cloud_parsing.description: Paid cloud service with proprietary models. Agentic OCR, structured outputs (Markdown, JSON schemas), premium accuracy on dense tables, charts, and handwritten text. Built for production document intelligence.

framework.description: The broader Python/TS framework for building LLM applications. LiteParse slots in as the document loading stage — a drop-in component for VectorStoreIndex and IngestionPipeline workflows.

use_when: You're building AI agents that need to read documents quickly and move on. Speed matters more than perfect structural conversion.
You want local-first execution. Your documents are sensitive, your environment is air-gapped, or you simply don't want to pay for cloud parsing on straightforward documents.
Your stack is JavaScript/TypeScript. LiteParse is native to this environment — no Python runtime overhead, installs via npm in seconds.
You need a two-step parse-then-screenshot workflow. LiteParse was built around exactly this agent pattern: fast text first, visual fallback second.
skip_when: Your documents have dense, complex tables with merged cells, multi-level headers, or columns that don't snap to a clean grid. Spatial parsing alone won't resolve these reliably.
You need structured output — Markdown tables, JSON schemas, strict key-value extraction. LiteParse outputs spatial text and bounding boxes. That's it.
You're processing handwritten text or heavily degraded scans. Built-in Tesseract.js is decent for standard scans but isn't state-of-the-art OCR. You'll want LlamaParse or a dedicated OCR model.
You need chart or diagram parsing — extracting data from visual elements. This requires multimodal LLM reasoning on screenshots, which LiteParse can enable but doesn't do itself.

use_cases.title: Two-Step Document Reading
use_cases.description: Agents parse text first for fast understanding, then generate page screenshots for visual follow-up on charts or complex layouts. LiteParse ships as an agent skill for this exact pattern.

use_cases.title: Local Document Ingestion
use_cases.description: Feed documents into a vector store without cloud round-trips. LiteParse handles the parsing stage of RAG pipelines where latency and privacy matter — internal docs, legal files, financial reports.

use_cases.title: CLI Document Processing
use_cases.description: Pipe remote PDFs directly through LiteParse from the command line. Batch-parse entire directories. Integrate into CI/CD or automation scripts without standing up a service.

use_cases.title: Air-Gapped Environments
use_cases.description: Regulated industries (finance, healthcare, government) where documents cannot leave the network. LiteParse runs fully offline with no external calls — OCR included.

use_cases.title: Text + Vision Workflows
use_cases.description: Combine spatial text extraction with page screenshots. Feed both into multimodal models for richer document understanding — particularly useful for reports with embedded charts and diagrams.

use_cases.title: Browser & Edge Parsing
use_cases.description: TypeScript-native means LiteParse fits into web-based and edge-computing environments without a Python runtime. Parse documents closer to the user, closer to the data.

timeline.title: LlamaIndex Launches
timeline.detail: Originally called GPT Index, the framework establishes itself as the go-to toolkit for connecting LLMs to external data sources. Document loading is a core concern from day one.

timeline.title: LlamaParse Goes to Production
timeline.detail: LlamaIndex launches LlamaParse as a managed cloud parsing service. Agentic OCR, structured outputs, and premium accuracy — built specifically for enterprise document intelligence pipelines.

timeline.title: LiteParse Open-Sourced
timeline.detail: LlamaIndex extracts the lightweight, fast-mode core of LlamaParse's parsing engine and releases it as LiteParse — standalone open-source under Apache-2.0. TypeScript-native, zero cloud dependencies, designed for AI agents.

timeline.title: 2.8k GitHub Stars in Two Weeks
timeline.detail: Rapid community adoption. LiteParse reaches version 1.3.1 with Python wrapper, Homebrew formula, and agent skill packaging. Repo includes benchmarking code and evaluation datasets on HuggingFace.
